All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (1) high PaO2 does NOT reverse CO binding, refuted by the measured 32% COHb; (2) CO shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFTWARD (not rightward) and impairs O2 offloading, worsening delivery; (3) methemoglobin (which pins SpO2 ~85%) is not present here; (4) motion/low-perfusion artifact degrades the signal rather than producing a consistent falsely reassuring 98%. Rationales and the explanation are accurate and current. Minor pedantic note (not an error): at high COHb the pulse ox classically plateaus near ~90% rather than tracking linearly to 100%, so a reading of exactly 98% is on the generous end — but the conceptual point and the answer remain correct and unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470348/ resolves to StatPearls "Pulse Oximetry," which explicitly covers the 660/940 nm two-wavelength technology and states that carboxyhemoglobinemia changes the absorption spectrum and leads to false readings requiring co-oximeter confirmation. The citation is valid and directly on-topic. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 6-year-old boy is the first case on a Monday morning at an outpatient surgery center. The anesthesia machine was left with high fresh-gas flows (10 L/min) running over the weekend by mistake. The child is induced and maintained on sevoflurane in oxygen. Roughly 40 minutes into the case, the AA observes that the CO2 absorbent canister is unusually hot to the touch and notes a slowly rising inspired CO2 baseline on the capnogram despite fresh granules having been loaded. Which mechanism BEST explains the heat and the risk introduced by the weekend gas flow?

choices.text: Desiccation of the absorbent by prolonged high fresh-gas flow, promoting exothermic degradation of sevoflurane and carbon monoxide / Compound A production
choices.rationale: Correct — continuous high-flow gas dries out the absorbent, and the reaction of volatile anesthetic with strong-base desiccated granules is exothermic and generates carbon monoxide (and Compound A with sevoflurane).

choices.text: Saturation of the absorbent with water from normal CO2 neutralization, which is an endothermic process
choices.rationale: Incorrect — CO2 neutralization is exothermic and produces water; a fully hydrated canister does not desiccate or overheat in this way, and the scenario describes drying, not saturation.

choices.text: Channeling of gas around fresh granules, which lowers canister temperature and is harmless
choices.rationale: Incorrect — channeling causes early CO2 breakthrough but does not generate the marked exothermic heat or the carbon monoxide hazard described.

choices.text: Excess water vapor catalyzing conversion of sevoflurane to fluoride ions in the canister
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the hazard is driven by absent (not excess) water; desiccation, not hydration, is what promotes the degradative reactions.

choices.text: Reaction of oxygen with nickel in the canister housing producing localized exothermic corrosion
choices.rationale: Incorrect — there is no such oxygen-nickel reaction in a CO2 absorber; the heat arises from anesthetic degradation on desiccated strong-base absorbent.

explanation: When fresh-gas flow runs continuously at high rates (e.g., a machine left on over a weekend), the CO2 absorbent becomes desiccated. Dry absorbent containing strong bases (NaOH/KOH) reacts exothermically with volatile anesthetics, generating heat that can be felt on the canister and, with sevoflurane, producing carbon monoxide and Compound A; severe cases have caused canister fires and patient CO exposure. Clues include an unexpectedly hot absorber and a rising inspired CO2 baseline. Prevention includes turning off gas flow when the machine is not in use and replacing absorbent suspected of being desiccated.
subtopic: Breathing circuit & CO2 absorbent chemistry — desiccated absorbent, carbon monoxide and Compound A

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best, unambiguous choice. Prolonged high fresh-gas flow desiccates the CO2 absorbent; dry strong-base granules (NaOH/KOH) react exothermically with volatile anesthetic, generating canister heat (well-documented to cause fires) and carbon monoxide — the classic "first case Monday after weekend flows" scenario, with a rising inspired CO2 baseline as a clue. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: index 1 falsely labels CO2 neutralization endothermic (it is exothermic) and misframes saturation; index 2 (channeling) does not produce marked heat/CO; index 3 (excess water → fluoride ions) inverts the cause (hazard is ABSENT water, and fluoride relates to renal metabolism, not canister chemistry); index 4 (oxygen-nickel corrosion) is fabricated. The explanation is accurate and current. Minor imprecision only: the keyed answer bundles "Compound A" with the desiccation/CO mechanism, whereas Compound A is primarily a sevoflurane low-flow degradation product that is a related but distinct phenomenon — not enough to make the key wrong or the item ambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK572060/ resolves to the real StatPearls "Anesthesia Machine" article, which is on-topic and explicitly covers the CO2 absorbent composition (calcium/sodium/potassium/barium hydroxides). It does not deep-dive desiccation hazards, but it is a legitimate, relevant, non-fabricated citation for the breathing circuit/absorbent subtopic.

stem: An 82-year-old, 58-kg woman is scheduled for an open reduction and internal fixation of a wrist fracture under general anesthesia. Her history includes hypertension and stage 3 chronic kidney disease. Compared with a healthy 30-year-old undergoing the same procedure, which adjustment in the anesthetic plan is MOST consistent with the expected age-related pharmacologic changes in this patient?

choices.text: Reduce the induction dose of propofol and titrate slowly because of increased pharmacodynamic sensitivity and decreased clearance
choices.rationale: Correct: aging increases brain sensitivity to propofol and reduces clearance and central volume, so a smaller, slowly titrated dose achieves the desired effect while limiting hypotension.

choices.text: Increase the age-adjusted MAC of sevoflurane because volatile anesthetic requirements rise with advancing age
choices.rationale: Incorrect: MAC decreases roughly 6% per decade after age 40, so the elderly require less volatile anesthetic, not more.

choices.text: Administer a proportionally larger initial fentanyl bolus because opioid receptors are downregulated in the elderly
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the elderly are more sensitive to opioids and require reduced doses, and there is no clinically useful opioid receptor downregulation justifying a larger bolus.

choices.text: Select an induction dose of thiopental on a strict mg/kg basis identical to that used in young adults
choices.rationale: Incorrect: reduced central compartment volume and slower redistribution mean barbiturate doses must be reduced in the elderly rather than scaled identically by weight.

choices.text: Expect faster emergence from a prolonged sevoflurane anesthetic because of reduced body fat stores
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the elderly typically have an increased fat fraction that stores lipid-soluble volatile agents and tends to prolong, not speed, emergence.

explanation: Aging produces predictable pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic changes: a smaller central compartment, decreased hepatic and renal clearance, increased body fat, and heightened end-organ sensitivity of the aging brain. The net effect for most induction agents (propofol, thiopental), opioids, and volatile anesthetics (MAC falls ~6% per decade) is that LESS drug is needed and it should be titrated slowly. Failing to dose-reduce predisposes the elderly patient to profound hypotension and delayed emergence.
subtopic: Age-related pharmacology and dose adjustment
reference_title: OpenAnesthesia – Pharmacological Changes with Aging: Inhalational and Intravenous Anesthetics

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (choice 0) is unambiguously the single best answer. Reducing the propofol induction dose and titrating slowly is the cornerstone of geriatric anesthetic dosing, driven by both increased pharmacodynamic brain sensitivity (~30% greater) and decreased clearance/reduced central compartment volume — all accurate. All four distractors assert the OPPOSITE of established physiology and are genuinely inferior: (1) MAC decreases ~6%/decade after 40, it does not rise; (2) the elderly are MORE opioid-sensitive and require reduced doses — there is no clinically useful receptor downregulation justifying a larger bolus; (3) thiopental cannot be scaled identically by weight because reduced central compartment volume and slower redistribution mandate dose reduction; (4) the elderly have INCREASED fat fraction, which stores lipid-soluble agents and tends to prolong, not speed, emergence. The explanation (smaller central compartment, decreased hepatic/renal clearance, increased body fat, heightened brain sensitivity, MAC falls ~6%/decade) is accurate and current. The stem's HTN and stage 3 CKD reinforce reduced clearance and do not create a competing answer. No ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves successfully; the page is titled exactly as cited and is directly on-topic, explicitly confirming the propofol sensitivity (~30%), propofol bolus reduction 40-50%, MAC decrease ~6%/decade, and increased body fat (50-70%) claims. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 79-year-old man with mild baseline cognitive impairment, controlled hypertension, and a remote history of stroke is undergoing repair of a fall-related hip fracture. The surgical and anesthesia teams want to minimize his risk of postoperative delirium. Which intraoperative or perioperative strategy is BEST supported as reducing this risk?

choices.text: Administer a scheduled prophylactic benzodiazepine infusion to keep him calm in the postoperative period
choices.rationale: Incorrect: benzodiazepines are a well-recognized precipitant of delirium in older adults and should be minimized, not given prophylactically.

choices.text: Avoid processed EEG monitoring so that a deeper, steadier anesthetic plane can be maintained throughout the case
choices.rationale: Incorrect: avoiding depth monitoring promotes excessively deep anesthesia, which is associated with more delirium, and EEG-guided titration is intended to prevent that.

choices.text: Limit the cumulative dose of sedating and anticholinergic medications and avoid excessive anesthetic depth
choices.rationale: Correct: minimizing deliriogenic drugs (benzodiazepines, anticholinergics) and avoiding overly deep anesthesia are core, evidence-supported measures to lower postoperative delirium risk.

choices.text: Routinely add scopolamine for postoperative nausea prophylaxis given his stroke history
choices.rationale: Incorrect: scopolamine is a centrally acting anticholinergic that increases delirium risk in the elderly and should generally be avoided in this population.

choices.text: Maintain deep sedation in the PACU and defer reorientation, hearing aids, and glasses until the next morning
choices.rationale: Incorrect: withholding sensory aids and reorientation removes protective nonpharmacologic measures and worsens, rather than reduces, delirium risk.

explanation: Postoperative delirium is the most common neurologic complication in older surgical patients and is independently linked to increased morbidity and mortality. The strongest modifiable anesthetic levers are pharmacologic restraint—minimizing benzodiazepines and anticholinergics—and avoiding excessive anesthetic depth, often guided by processed EEG. Nonpharmacologic bundle elements such as early reorientation and restoring glasses and hearing aids further reduce risk and should be started promptly.
subtopic: Postoperative delirium prevention

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2 — limit cumulative sedating/anticholinergic drugs and avoid excessive anesthetic depth) is the single best, consensus-supported strategy for reducing postoperative delirium in an older patient. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and actively deliriogenic or counterproductive: prophylactic benzodiazepine infusion (major precipitant), avoiding pEEG to deepen anesthesia (deeper anesthesia associates with more delirium), routine scopolamine (central anticholinergic), and deferring reorientation/sensory aids (removes protective nonpharmacologic measures). Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. Minor caveat: EEG-guided depth has trial heterogeneity (ENGAGES negative), but the stem frames pEEG as a helper, not the sole basis, so no ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534831/ resolves to the genuine StatPearls 'Postoperative Delirium' article, matching reference_title exactly and covering benzodiazepine/anticholinergic avoidance, light vs deep sedation, and nonpharmacologic measures. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: An 84-year-old, frail woman (BMI 19 kg/m^2) is 40 minutes into a 3-hour open abdominal procedure under general anesthesia with sevoflurane. A forced-air warmer was placed only after draping. Her esophageal temperature has fallen from 36.6°C to 35.4°C. Which mechanism explains the LARGEST portion of this early intraoperative temperature drop, and what is the most appropriate physiologic interpretation in this elderly patient?

choices.text: Anesthetic-induced internal redistribution of heat from the warm core to the cooler periphery, exaggerated by her impaired thermoregulation and low body mass
choices.rationale: Correct: the largest early drop after induction is core-to-peripheral redistribution from vasodilation, and the elderly are especially vulnerable because of reduced thermoregulatory defenses and limited insulating tissue.

choices.text: A primary increase in metabolic heat production that has overwhelmed her capacity to dissipate it
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anesthesia lowers metabolic heat production and impairs thermoregulation, so increased heat generation does not explain the temperature fall.

choices.text: Evaporative loss from the open abdomen, which is the dominant cause of the temperature change within the first hour
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while evaporative and radiant losses contribute over time, internal redistribution dominates the characteristic rapid first-hour drop.

choices.text: An intact, robust vasoconstrictive response that is transiently sequestering heat in the extremities
choices.rationale: Incorrect: general anesthesia abolishes the vasoconstriction threshold rather than enhancing it, and elderly patients have blunted vasoconstriction to begin with.

choices.text: A laboratory artifact, because esophageal probes systematically overestimate the magnitude of core cooling
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the distal esophageal probe is a reliable core-temperature monitor, and the decline reflects true redistribution hypothermia rather than artifact.

explanation: The rapid 1–1.5°C fall in the first hour of general anesthesia is driven mainly by redistribution of heat from the warm central core to vasodilated peripheral tissues, because anesthetics lower the vasoconstriction threshold and abolish behavioral defenses. Heat production, insulation, and thermoregulatory capacity all decline with age, and a frail, low-BMI elderly patient has little insulating tissue, magnifying the drop. Prewarming and forced-air warming before redistribution occurs is the most effective prevention; once hypothermia develops it predisposes to dysrhythmias, coagulopathy, surgical site infection, and delayed wound healing.
subtopic: Perioperative thermoregulation and hypothermia
reference_title: StatPearls – Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (option 0, anesthetic-induced core-to-peripheral redistribution of heat) is the single best answer — this is well-established Sessler physiology: the rapid 1–1.5°C core temperature fall in the first hour of GA is predominantly due to redistribution from vasodilation and lowering of the vasoconstriction threshold. The stem's 1.2°C drop at 40 minutes fits this timeframe precisely. All distractors are genuinely inferior: increased metabolic heat (wrong, GA reduces metabolic rate); evaporative loss as dominant first-hour cause (good distractor, but redistribution dominates the early drop); intact/robust vasoconstriction (wrong, GA impairs vasoconstriction and elderly responses are blunted); lab artifact (wrong, distal esophageal probe is a reliable core monitor). Rationales and the explanation are accurate and current, including the elderly-specific points about reduced thermoregulatory defenses and insulating tissue, and downstream complications. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK572137/ resolves to StatPearls 'Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population,' which is on-topic — it explicitly states heat production, insulation, and thermoregulatory ability decline with age and recommends normothermia to reduce dysrhythmias, infection, and delayed wound healing, closely matching the explanation. Both clinical and reference are sound; keep=true.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman is undergoing an uncomplicated cesarean delivery under a well-functioning spinal anesthetic. Moments after delivery of the placenta, she suddenly becomes acutely dyspneic and anxious, complaining that she 'can't breathe,' then loses consciousness. Her SpO2 falls to 70%, blood pressure drops to 60/30 mmHg, and the monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 150/min. Shortly afterward the surgical field begins oozing diffusely and bleeding from the uterine incision will not clot. There is no rash, wheezing, or facial edema. Which of the following best explains this clinical picture?

choices.text: High spinal blockade causing respiratory muscle paralysis and sympathectomy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a high spinal can cause hypotension and dyspnea, but it does not produce the abrupt profound hypoxemia followed by a consumptive coagulopathy with uncontrolled surgical bleeding seen here.

choices.text: Amniotic fluid embolism
choices.rationale: Correct: the sudden peripartum triad of cardiorespiratory collapse with profound hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, and disseminated intravascular coagulation occurring around the time of delivery is the hallmark presentation of amniotic fluid embolism.

choices.text: Anaphylaxis to a perioperative medication
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anaphylaxis typically presents with bronchospasm, urticaria, or angioedema and is not characteristically accompanied by an immediate consumptive coagulopathy, both of which make AFE more likely here.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from the spinal anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect: spinal doses of local anesthetic are far too small to cause systemic toxicity, and LAST does not produce a fibrinolytic coagulopathy with uncontrolled uterine bleeding.

choices.text: Acute hemorrhagic shock from concealed uterine atony
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while hemorrhage can cause hypotension and tachycardia, it does not by itself explain the sudden severe hypoxemia and collapse that preceded the bleeding, whereas AFE causes both the collapse and the secondary coagulopathy.

explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency presenting as sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxemic respiratory failure, and disseminated intravascular coagulation around the time of labor, delivery, or the immediate postpartum period. The pathophysiology is thought to be an anaphylactoid/inflammatory response to fetal antigens entering the maternal circulation, producing acute pulmonary vasoconstriction, right heart failure, and activation of the coagulation cascade. Management is supportive and immediate: high-quality CPR if arrest occurs, securing the airway, hemodynamic support with vasopressors and inotropes, and aggressive correction of coagulopathy with blood products (including fibrinogen/cryoprecipitate). It is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion, and prompt recognition with a multidisciplinary response is critical to survival.
subtopic: Amniotic fluid embolism
reference_title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL (sound): The stem is a textbook amniotic fluid embolism presentation — peripartum onset just after placental delivery, the classic triad of profound hypoxemia, sudden cardiovascular collapse, and a consumptive coagulopathy (non-clotting uterine bleeding/DIC), with rash/wheeze/edema explicitly absent to exclude anaphylaxis. Keyed answer B is the single best answer and is unambiguous. All distractors are genuinely inferior: high spinal explains hypotension/dyspnea but not abrupt hypoxemia plus DIC; anaphylaxis is excluded by the absent cutaneous/bronchospastic signs and is not characterized by immediate DIC; LAST is implausible at spinal doses and causes no coagulopathy; hemorrhagic shock from atony cannot explain the antecedent hypoxemia/collapse. The explanation (anaphylactoid/inflammatory response to fetal antigens, pulmonary vasoconstriction, RV failure, coagulation cascade activation, supportive management with vasopressors/inotropes and blood products including fibrinogen/cryoprecipitate, clinical diagnosis of exclusion) is accurate and current.\n\nREFERENCE (broken/mismatched): I WebFetched the reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470225/. It resolves but the page is titled 'Shellfish and Dinoflagellate Toxicity' — completely unrelated to amniotic fluid embolism or obstetrics. The reference_title claims it is the AFE StatPearls chapter, but the book ID NBK470225 maps to shellfish toxicity. Verified twice. This is a fabricated/mismatched citation. Fix: replace with the correct StatPearls AFE chapter URL (the cited ID NBK470225 and adjacent IDs do not point to the AFE article).

stem: A 58-year-old man (BMI 41) presents to a freestanding ambulatory surgery center for elective open inguinal hernia repair. He reports loud snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, and treated hypertension; his neck circumference is 44 cm. He has never had a sleep study and is not on CPAP. His STOP-Bang screen is positive on 6 of 8 items. Vitals are stable and the airway exam is Mallampati III. Which decision is most consistent with safe patient selection for this freestanding (non-hospital-affiliated) facility?

choices.text: Proceed at the freestanding center with a general anesthetic and discharge home when standard recovery criteria are met
choices.rationale: A high STOP-Bang score with comorbidities and an opioid-requiring procedure raises the risk of postoperative respiratory depression beyond what a freestanding center with limited rescue resources should accept.

choices.text: Cancel surgery permanently until the patient completes a formal polysomnogram and is established on CPAP
choices.rationale: Routine mandatory polysomnography before all surgery is not required and would inappropriately delay needed care; the issue is matching the site of care to risk, not indefinite cancellation.

choices.text: Proceed only with monitored anesthesia care and assume sedation eliminates the perioperative airway risk of presumed OSA
choices.rationale: Sedation does not eliminate OSA-related risk and can worsen upper-airway collapse and apnea, so MAC is not inherently a safe substitute at an under-resourced site.

choices.text: Reschedule the procedure at a hospital-based or hospital-affiliated facility capable of extended postoperative monitoring, optimizing comorbidities and planning opioid-sparing analgesia
choices.rationale: Correct: a patient with a high probability of moderate-to-severe untreated OSA and additional risk factors undergoing an opioid-requiring procedure is best managed where prolonged monitoring and rescue capability exist.

choices.text: Proceed at the freestanding center but commit to overnight observation in the recovery area
choices.rationale: Freestanding ambulatory centers are not designed or staffed for true overnight monitored admission, so this does not provide the appropriate level of postoperative surveillance.

explanation: STOP-Bang of 6 indicates high probability of moderate-to-severe OSA, and this patient is untreated, obese, hypertensive, and scheduled for a procedure likely to need postoperative opioids—an additive risk for postoperative respiratory depression. Patient selection for ambulatory surgery should match anticipated risk to the resources of the site; freestanding centers lack the extended monitoring and inpatient rescue capability appropriate for such patients. The optimal plan is to perform the case at a hospital-based or hospital-affiliated facility with capacity for prolonged monitoring while optimizing comorbidities and emphasizing opioid-sparing, multimodal analgesia.
subtopic: Patient and facility selection: obstructive sleep apnea screening (STOP-Bang)

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound. The keyed answer (index 3 — reschedule to a hospital-based/affiliated facility with extended monitoring, optimize comorbidities, opioid-sparing analgesia) is the single best answer. This patient has very high pre-test probability of moderate-to-severe untreated OSA (STOP-Bang 6/8, BMI 41, witnessed apneas, HTN, neck 44 cm) undergoing an opioid-requiring open inguinal hernia repair at a freestanding (non-hospital-affiliated) ASC. This matches the SAMBA consensus framework: known/suspected OSA patients are acceptable for ambulatory surgery only if comorbidities are optimized AND postop pain is manageable predominantly with non-opioid techniques — criteria not met here. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: proceed-and-discharge (0) accepts undue risk at an under-resourced site; permanent cancellation pending PSG/CPAP (1) is wrong because routine preop PSG is not mandated; MAC eliminating risk (2) rests on a false premise since sedation worsens upper-airway collapse; overnight observation in the recovery area (3) is invalid because freestanding ASCs are not licensed/staffed for true overnight admission. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current.

REFERENCE: Broken/mismatched. WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/obstructive-sleep-apnea/ resolves (not a 404) but the page is titled 'Obstructive Sleep Apnea: Pathophysiology' and covers only endotypes/phenotypes and disease mechanisms. It contains nothing on STOP-Bang scoring, ambulatory patient selection, or facility/site-of-care selection — the actual subject being tested. The citation does not support the question's teaching point, so it is an off-topic reference mismatch.

stem: A 68-year-old man with hypertension and a 40-pack-year smoking history is undergoing transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) under spinal anesthesia. Approximately 70 minutes into the resection, with glycine irrigation fluid hung 80 cm above the patient, he becomes restless and complains of nausea and visual disturbance described as a transient bluish haze. Vitals show BP 168/96 mmHg, HR 52/min, and SpO2 96%. A stat serum sodium returns at 118 mEq/L. Which feature of this presentation is MOST specifically attributable to the irrigant chosen for this procedure rather than to fluid absorption alone?

choices.text: Transient visual disturbance and blindness
choices.rationale: Correct: glycine is metabolized to ammonia and glyoxylic acid and acts as an inhibitory retinal neurotransmitter, producing the transient visual symptoms and even blindness that are characteristic of glycine irrigation specifically.

choices.text: Dilutional hyponatremia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hyponatremia results from absorption of any large-volume electrolyte-free irrigant and is not specific to glycine.

choices.text: Reflex bradycardia and hypertension
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this reflects volume overload and the body's response to acute hypervolemia, which occurs with any near-isotonic or hypotonic irrigant absorbed in large volume.

choices.text: Restlessness and altered mental status
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cerebral edema from hypo-osmolality causes these symptoms regardless of which electrolyte-free irrigant is used.

choices.text: Nausea and vomiting
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nausea is a nonspecific early sign of TURP syndrome driven by hyponatremia and hypervolemia rather than by glycine itself.

explanation: TURP syndrome arises from systemic absorption of electrolyte-free irrigant through opened prostatic venous sinuses, producing dilutional hyponatremia, hypo-osmolality, and hypervolemia. Glycine irrigant adds a unique toxicity: glycine is an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the retina, and its metabolism to ammonia can cause transient visual disturbance, a halo or bluish haze, and even temporary blindness. Risk is reduced by limiting resection time (ideally under 60 minutes) and keeping the irrigation bag no more than 60 cm above the patient to limit hydrostatic absorption pressure.
subtopic: TURP syndrome and irrigation fluid toxicity
reference_title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL (sound): The keyed answer is correct and unambiguous. Transient visual disturbance / "TURP blindness" is the one feature genuinely specific to glycine, which acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the retina; this is distinct from hyponatremia, hypervolemia, reflex bradycardia/hypertension, cerebral edema, and nausea, all of which occur with ANY electrolyte-free irrigant (water, sorbitol, mannitol, glycine). The stem cleanly asks which feature is attributable to the irrigant CHOSEN rather than absorption alone, and only the visual symptom fits, so all four distractors are genuinely inferior. Minor (non-disqualifying) nuance: the winning rationale partly attributes the visual symptoms to ammonia metabolism, whereas the retinal effect is chiefly glycine's direct inhibitory neurotransmitter action — both real, conclusion unchanged. REFERENCE (broken/mismatched): WebFetch of the cited reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538142/ resolves but returns the StatPearls chapter titled "Bland Diet (Archived)", which has nothing to do with TURP syndrome, glycine, or irrigation toxicity. The citation is mismatched/fabricated relative to reference_title ("Transurethral Resection of the Prostate Syndrome (StatPearls)"). Because clinical content is correct but the citation is wrong, this is a reference issue: keep=false, clinically_sound=true. Fix by repointing to the correct StatPearls TURP-syndrome chapter.

stem: A 71-year-old woman with osteoporosis and a long history of well-controlled rheumatoid arthritis presents for cemented hemiarthroplasty of a displaced femoral neck fracture under general anesthesia. Shortly after the surgeon pressurizes the cement and impacts the femoral prosthesis, the SpO2 falls from 99% to 84%, end-tidal CO2 drops abruptly from 38 to 22 mmHg, and the noninvasive blood pressure reads 72/40 mmHg with a heart rate of 124/min. The airway pressures are unchanged and breath sounds are clear and symmetric. Which underlying mechanism BEST explains this constellation of findings?

choices.text: Anaphylaxis to the methylmethacrylate monomer with histamine-mediated vasodilation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: true IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to cement is rare and would typically include bronchospasm, elevated airway pressures, or cutaneous signs, which are absent here.

choices.text: Tension pneumothorax from positive-pressure ventilation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a tension pneumothorax would produce elevated airway pressures and asymmetric or absent breath sounds, neither of which is present.

choices.text: Bone cement implantation syndrome with embolization of marrow debris and acute pulmonary hypertension
choices.rationale: Correct: pressurization and prosthesis impaction force fat, marrow, and air emboli into the venous circulation, causing acute pulmonary hypertension and right heart strain that produce the sudden drop in end-tidal CO2, hypoxemia, and hypotension characteristic of bone cement implantation syndrome.

choices.text: Acute hemorrhagic shock from femoral canal reaming
choices.rationale: Incorrect: blood loss causes hypotension and tachycardia but would not produce the abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 from increased dead space that points to embolic obstruction of pulmonary flow.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from periarticular infiltration
choices.rationale: Incorrect: LAST typically presents with neurologic prodrome and then cardiac arrhythmia rather than an isolated abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 coincident with cementing.

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) is characterized by hypoxia, hypotension, and cardiac arrhythmias or arrest occurring around the time of cementation, prosthesis insertion, or reduction. The leading mechanism is embolization of marrow fat, air, and cement debris into the pulmonary circulation during canal pressurization, causing acute pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular dysfunction; the abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 reflects increased alveolar dead space from obstructed pulmonary blood flow. Risk-reduction strategies include adequate hydration, maintaining high inspired oxygen during cementing, surgical lavage and venting of the femoral canal, and close communication so the anesthetist anticipates the high-risk moment of prosthesis impaction.
subtopic: Bone cement implantation syndrome in arthroplasty
reference_title: Methyl Methacrylate Complications and Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Clinical content is fully sound: the vignette is a textbook BCIS presentation (abrupt hypoxemia, sudden EtCO2 fall from increased alveolar dead space, hypotension/tachycardia at the moment of cement pressurization and prosthesis impaction during cemented hemiarthroplasty for a femoral neck fracture — the highest-risk scenario). Keyed answer (index 2) is unambiguously best; all distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly refuted (anaphylaxis lacks bronchospasm/airway-pressure/cutaneous signs; tension pneumothorax would raise airway pressures and give asymmetric breath sounds; hemorrhage would not abruptly drop EtCO2; LAST has a neurologic prodrome and wrong mechanism). Explanation and risk-reduction measures (hydration, high FiO2, canal lavage/venting, communication at impaction) are accurate and current. However, the reference fails: reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK573078/ is labeled "Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome (StatPearls)" but actually resolves to the StatPearls chapter on "Stiff Person Syndrome," an unrelated autoimmune neurologic disorder. Confirmed via two independent WebFetch calls — the NBK ID is wrong and the citation is a mismatch. Fix by repointing to the correct BCIS source.
